configuration/outlook-configuration.html.title: Outlook Address Book Configuration | User Docs
configuration/outlook-configuration.html.summary: Outlook Address Book Configuration This guide explains how to configure the Outlook address book provider to fetch contacts from Microsoft Outlook. Overview The Outlook provider allows you to retrieve contact information from Outlook address books and use it within Word documents for mail merge, data insertion, or other purposes. Prerequisites Microsoft Outlook must be installed on the system Outlook must be configured with at least one profile The add-in must have permission to access Outlook (check Outlook Trust Center settings) Configuration Basic Configuration Add the following configuration to your dataset configuration file: { "$schema": "../../../output/schemas/DatasetProviderConfiguration.schema.json", "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "OutlookContacts": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 30, "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Name", "EmailAddress": "Email", "CompanyName": "Company", "JobTitle": "Title", "BusinessPhone": "Phone", "MobilePhone": "Mobile" }, Required Properties $connectionType Value: "Outlook" Description: Specifies that this dataset uses the Outlook provider OutlookAddressbook Type: string Required: Yes Description: The name of the Outlook address book to access Common Values: "Contacts" - Your personal contacts folder "Global Address List" - Organization's GAL (Exchange environments) Custom address book names configured in Outlook ConnectionSettings Type: string Description: Reserved for future use (currently unused but required) Default: "" SQLQuery Type: string Description: Reserved for future filtering capabilities Default: "" Optional Properties Timeout Type: integer Default: 30 Description: Maximum time in seconds to wait for Outlook operations FieldAliasOrder Type: Dictionary<string, string> Description: Maps internal field names to friendly display names Available Fields: FullName - Complete name from address book FirstName - First name (if available) LastName - Last name (if available) EmailAddress - Email address CompanyName - Company/organization name JobTitle - Position/title BusinessPhone - Business telephone number MobilePhone - Mobile phone number BusinessAddress - Business address Type - Entry type (Contact, Exchange User, etc.) UseLocalCache Type: boolean Default: false Description: Enable caching to improve performance for large address books LocalCachePath Type: string Description: Path for cached data (supports environment variables) Example: "%temp%\\outlook_cache" Examples Example 1: Personal Contacts { "PersonalContacts": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 30, "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Contact Name", "EmailAddress": "Email", "CompanyName": "Company", "BusinessPhone": "Phone" } } } Example 2: Global Address List (Exchange) { "CompanyDirectory": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 60, "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Employee Name", "EmailAddress": "Email Address", "JobTitle": "Position", "CompanyName": "Department" }, "UseLocalCache": true, "LocalCachePath": "%localappdata%\\KutanaBrochet\\Cache" } } Example 3: Multiple Address Books { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "Clients": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Client Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "" }, "Staff": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Company Staff", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "" }, "External": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "" } } } } Finding Your Address Book Names To find the exact names of your Outlook address books: Open Microsoft Outlook Click on Home tab > Address Book button (or press Ctrl+Shift+B) In the Address Book window, use the dropdown at the top The dropdown shows all available address book names - use these exact names in your configuration Common address book names: Contacts - Your personal Outlook contacts Global Address List - Company directory (Exchange/Microsoft 365) Suggested Contacts - Auto-collected contacts Any custom address books you've created Returned Data Structure The Outlook provider returns a DataSet with the following columns: Column Name Data Type Description FullName string Complete display name FirstName string First name (when available) LastName string Last name (when available) EmailAddress string Primary email address CompanyName string Company/organization name JobTitle string Job title/position BusinessPhone string Business telephone number MobilePhone string Mobile phone number BusinessAddress string Business address Type string Entry type identifier Note: Not all fields may be populated for every contact, depending on what information is available in Outlook. Troubleshooting "Microsoft Outlook is not installed" Ensure Microsoft Outlook is installed on the system Verify that Outlook is properly licensed "Address book 'X' not found" Check the exact spelling of the address book name (case-sensitive) Open Outlook's Address Book (Ctrl+Shift+B) to verify available names Ensure you have permission to access the address book "Access denied to Outlook" Open Outlook Go to File > Options > Trust Center > Trust Center Settings Select Programmatic Access Ensure appropriate settings allow programmatic access You may need to approve access when prompted "No entries found in address book" Verify the address book contains contacts Check if you have read permissions for the address book For Exchange GAL, ensure you're connected to the server Performance Issues with Large Address Books Enable UseLocalCache in configuration Increase the Timeout value Consider filtering contacts (future feature via SQLQuery) Security Considerations The add-in requires programmatic access to Outlook Outlook may prompt users to grant access when first connecting Cached data is stored locally - ensure appropriate file permissions Consider data privacy when using Global Address Lists Cached data is not encrypted - use secure storage locations Best Practices Use Descriptive Dataset Names: Name datasets clearly (e.g., "ClientContacts", "StaffDirectory") Enable Caching for Large Lists: Use local cache for GAL or large contact lists Map Fields Appropriately: Use FieldAliasOrder to provide user-friendly column names Set Appropriate Timeouts: Increase timeout for large address books or slow networks Test with Small Address Books First: Verify configuration with personal contacts before using GAL Future Enhancements The following features are planned for future releases: SQLQuery Support: Filter contacts based on criteria Incremental Updates: Only fetch changed contacts Custom Field Mapping: Support for custom Outlook contact fields Multiple Profile Support: Specify which Outlook profile to use

configuration/outlook-implementation-sum.title: Outlook Address Book Integration - Implementation Summary | User Docs
configuration/outlook-implementation-sum.summary: Outlook Address Book Integration - Implementation Summary Overview I've successfully implemented the Outlook address book connection for your Office Add-in. The implementation allows you to fetch contacts from Microsoft Outlook address books and use them within Word documents for mail merge, data insertion, or other purposes. Changes Made 1. Updated OutlookProvider.cs Location: src\Core\Infrastructure.Persistence\ExternalData\Providers\Outlook.cs Key Implementation Details: COM Interop: Uses late binding (dynamic) to interact with Outlook's COM objects Multi-Framework Support: Conditional compilation for .NET Framework 4.8 and .NET 9 .NET 4.8: Uses Marshal.GetActiveObject to connect to running Outlook instance .NET 9: Uses Type.GetTypeFromProgID and Activator.CreateInstance Address Book Retrieval: Connects to Outlook via MAPI namespace Searches for the specified address book by name Retrieves all entries from the address book Data Extraction: Handles both Outlook Contacts and Exchange Users Extracts 10 fields: FullName, FirstName, LastName, EmailAddress, CompanyName, JobTitle, BusinessPhone, MobilePhone, BusinessAddress, Type Gracefully handles missing data (returns empty strings) COM Object Management: Properly releases all COM objects to prevent memory leaks Uses try-finally blocks to ensure cleanup Forces garbage collection after releasing COM objects Error Handling: Detailed error messages for common issues Handles: Outlook not installed, address book not found, access denied, COM exceptions Configuration Required Configuration Properties The OutlookDto class requires the following properties: { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", // Required: Name of the address book "ConnectionSettings": "", // Reserved for future use "SQLQuery": "", // Reserved for future filtering "Timeout": 30, // Optional: Timeout in seconds "FieldAliasOrder": { // Optional: Map column names "FullName": "Contact Name", "EmailAddress": "Email" }, "UseLocalCache": false, // Optional: Enable caching "LocalCachePath": "" // Optional: Cache file location } Configuration Examples 1. Basic Personal Contacts Configuration { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "MyContacts": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 30 } } } } 2. Global Address List (Exchange) { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "CompanyDirectory": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 60, "UseLocalCache": true, "LocalCachePath": "%temp%\\outlook_gal_cache" } } } } 3. Multiple Address Books with Field Mapping { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "Clients": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Client Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Client Name", "EmailAddress": "Email", "CompanyName": "Organization", "BusinessPhone": "Phone" } }, "Staff": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Employee", "EmailAddress": "Company Email", "JobTitle": "Position" } } } } } Data Structure The Outlook provider returns a DataSet with the following columns: Column Name Data Type Description FullName string Complete display name from address book FirstName string First name (when available from contact details) LastName string Last name (when available from contact details) EmailAddress string Primary email address CompanyName string Company/organization name JobTitle string Job title/position BusinessPhone string Business telephone number MobilePhone string Mobile phone number BusinessAddress string Business address Type string Entry type identifier Note: Not all fields may be populated for every contact, depending on what information is available in Outlook. How to Find Your Address Book Names Open Microsoft Outlook Click Home tab > Address Book button (or press Ctrl+Shift+B) In the Address Book window, use the dropdown at the top The dropdown shows all available address book names - use these exact names in your configuration Common address book names: Contacts - Your personal Outlook contacts Global Address List - Company directory (Exchange/Microsoft 365) Suggested Contacts - Auto-collected contacts Custom address books you've created Security Considerations Outlook Trust Center Users may need to configure Outlook to allow programmatic access: Open Outlook Go to File > Options > Trust Center > Trust Center Settings Select Programmatic Access Ensure appropriate settings allow programmatic access Users may need to approve access when first connecting (Outlook will prompt) Data Privacy Cached data is stored locally unencrypted - ensure secure storage locations Be mindful when using Global Address Lists - respect company data policies The add-in requires programmatic access to Outlook Troubleshooting "Microsoft Outlook is not installed" Solution: Install Microsoft Outlook on the system "Address book 'X' not found" Solution: Check exact spelling (case-sensitive), use Address Book dialog in Outlook to verify name "Access denied to Outlook" Solution: Configure Trust Center settings, approve access when prompted "No entries found in address book" Solution: Verify the address book contains contacts and you have read permissions Performance Issues with Large Address Books Solution: Enable UseLocalCache in configuration Increase the Timeout value (e.g., to 60 or 120 seconds) For GAL, consider using during off-peak hours Best Practices Descriptive Dataset Names: Use clear names like "ClientContacts", "StaffDirectory" Enable Caching for Large Lists: Use local cache for GAL or large contact lists to improve performance Field Mapping: Use FieldAliasOrder to provide user-friendly column names Appropriate Timeouts: Set timeout based on address book size: Small (< 100 contacts): 30 seconds (default) Medium (100-1000 contacts): 60 seconds Large (> 1000 contacts or GAL): 120+ seconds Test with Small Address Books First: Verify configuration with personal contacts before using GAL Future Enhancements The following features are reserved for future implementation: SQLQuery Support: Filter contacts based on criteria (currently reserved but not implemented) ConnectionSettings: Additional connection parameters (currently reserved) Incremental Updates: Only fetch changed contacts Custom Field Mapping: Support for custom Outlook contact fields Multiple Profile Support: Specify which Outlook profile to use Documentation I've created comprehensive documentation: Configuration Guide: docs/user/configuration/outlook-configuration.md Complete user documentation with examples Troubleshooting guide Security considerations Step-by-step instructions Example Configuration: docs/user/configuration/outlook-dataset-example.json Three complete examples: Personal contacts Global Address List Custom address book Testing Recommendations Unit Tests: Create tests for OutlookProvider.Create() validation Integration Tests: Test against a test Outlook profile with known contacts Performance Tests: Test with varying address book sizes to set appropriate timeouts Error Handling Tests: Test error scenarios: Outlook not running Invalid address book name Access denied Empty address book Usage Example After configuration, users can access Outlook contacts from your Word Add-in: // In your application code var query = new GetDataset.Query("MyContacts"); var result = queryDispatcher.Dispatch<GetDataset.Query, DataSet>(query); if (result.IsSuccess) { var contacts = result.Value.Tables[0]; // Use the contacts data... } Summary The Outlook address book integration is now complete and ready for use. The implementation: ✅ Connects to Microsoft Outlook via COM Interop ✅ Supports both .NET Framework 4.8 and .NET 9 ✅ Retrieves contacts from any Outlook address book ✅ Properly manages COM objects to prevent memory leaks ✅ Provides comprehensive error handling ✅ Supports field mapping and caching for performance ✅ Includes complete user documentation Users can now configure the add-in to pull contact data directly from their Outlook address books for use in Word documents.

configuration/ribbon.html.title: Ribbon | User Docs
configuration/ribbon.html.summary: Ribbon The file Ribbon.xml is used for the configuration of all the buttons on the Ribbon. Where this config file is located There can be a ribbon.xml file for each Office applications. There needs to be aseparate Ribbon.xml file for each application which is located in the following locations: %appdata%\Kutana+Brochet\<OfficeApp Name>\config eg ...\AppData\Roaming\Kutana+Brochet\Word\config or %localappdata%\Kutana+Brochet\<OfficeApp Name>\config The file located in the %localappdata% (..\Local\..) will take precedent on the one in %appdata% (..\Roaming\..). In this situation the file in Local will be used and the file in Roaming will be ignored. (see Using Configuration layering for the configuration files for more details on laying) XML Schema This file is written in XML and conforms the standard Microsoft Office Ribbon XML schema. The integration with Kutana+Brochet is through actions using the following five element parts: id image onAction getVisible getEnabled The Kutana+Brochet Coms addin contains a set of built in images. The list of images available can be seen in the following GitHub link: https://github.com/kutana-brochet/office-addins/tree/master/src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Resources Example style Button Below is the XML for a button on the ribbon for applying the style Level 1 Heading to the selected text: <button id="OperativeLevel1Heading" image="Level 1 Heading" onAction="RunScript" label="Level 1 Heading" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" screentip="Level 1 Heading - " supertip="Applies the first level heading style eg 1. in bold and ALLCAPS." /> Notes on each item: id="OperativeLevel1Heading" - this is only relevant when the onAction is "RunScript". This is used by the configuration setting "ScriptConfiguration" "Mappings"to define the commands to be run and when the button if visible and enabled. NB: The id needs to be unique for each ribbon element. To have two buttons that run the same script there will need to be a Mapping for each id but these can run the same Script. image="Level 1 Heading" - this is used to define the image to be used and displayed on the ribbon. The image can either be an inbuilt resource in the COMS addin or one generated by the system when Word is loaded. See image below https://github.com/kutana-brochet/office-addins/tree/master/src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Resources onAction="RunScript" - defines the command to be run. See onAction below for the for list of available commands getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" - this is only relvant when the onAction is "RunScript". The "ScriptConfiguration" "Mappings"to define the logic of when the button is visible. getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" - this is only relvant when the onAction is "RunScript". The "ScriptConfiguration" "Mappings"to define the logic of when the button is enables. image In the ribbon XML defined in a Word template any ribbon icon used in the image line needs to be added as a Resource to the Word template file. When using the Kutana+Brochet COMs addin these ribbon icons can either be added as a Resource in the COMSs addin for generated on the fly when Word is loaded. The embedded image incons The embedded list of icons can be found using the following link: https://github.com/kutana-brochet/office-addins/tree/master/src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Resources or they can be seen on the following Dropdown area: The list of icons embebbed on version ??? are: C:\Brochet Dropbox\Brochet internal\Development\Graphics\20251202 - MSI V7 Ribbon - Blob.png - BodyText1.png - BodyText1Blue.png - BodyText2.png [To be completed] Generating icons when Word is load Icon images can also be generated when Word is loaded.

features/RibbonIcons.html.title: RibbonIcons | User Docs
features/RibbonIcons.html.summary: RibbonIcons "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json" Description The JSON element "RibbonIcons" is used for defining the icons that are auto-generated when Word loads. The is one elements of "RibbonIcons": "Icons" Recommended JSON file name It is recommended that the JSON file holding this information is called StyleButtonsConfig.json. This section of the configuration can be divided between two separate files eg StyleButtonsConfigA.json and StyleButtonsConfigB.json and then will both be read and the information joined together. Remember if there is a "Icons" that is defined in both files the "RibbonIcons" in the filename that in later in the alapbetic list StyleButtonsConfigB.json will overwrite the other. "RibbonIcons" This section is used for defining the icon image. Example Below is an example of two Mappings for the Party 1 and Background 1 styles buttons: XML from the Ribbon.xml file: <box id="PartyBox1" boxStyle="vertical"> <button id="PartyParties" image="Parties 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Parties" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> <button id="PartyBackground" image="Background 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Background" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> </box> JSON from the configuration file StyleButtonsConfig.json Note: There are two settings for each icon the second being the same icon name except it has Dark appended to the name. This creates a black version of the same icons which is used when running Office in dark mode. Usually the ony difference between the two settings are the FontColour for example it is changed to White for dark mode. { "RibbonIcons": { "Icons": [ { "Name": "Parties 1", "Text": "(1)", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "Green", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 0, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, { "Name": "Parties 1Dark", "Text": "(1)", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "White", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 0, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, { "Name": "Background 1", "Text": "(A)", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "Green", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 2, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, { "Name": "Background 1Dark", "Text": "P", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "White", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 23, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, The following 11 elements are available for a ribbon icon: "Contrast" - not sure what this is but there are two setting 30 and 50. Think this is redundant!! "FontColour" - this is the list of colours that are available. The list of colour can be found in ???? How do we apply Hex colours "FontName" "FontSize" "FontStyle" "Name" "Size" "Text" "TextRenderingHint" "XOffset" "YOffset"

features/ScriptConfiguration.html.title: ScriptConfiguration | User Docs
features/ScriptConfiguration.html.summary: ScriptConfiguration "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json" Description The JSON element "ScriptConfiguration" is used for defining the "Scripts" that are run by ribbon buttons. These "Scripts" need to be linked back to the ribbon button by the "Mappings" section. The two elements of "ScriptConfiguration" are: "Mappings" "Scripts" Recommended JSON file name It is recommended that the JSON file holding this information is called Scripts.json. This section of the configuration can be divided between two separate files eg ScriptsStyleButtons.json and ScriptsMacroButtons.json and then will both be read and the information joined together. Remember if there is a "Mapping" that is defined in both files the "Mapping" in the filename that in later in the alapbetic list ScriptsStyleButtons.json will overwrite the other. "Mappings" This section is used for linking the ribbon elements (usually buttons) to the "Script" that will be run when the button is pressed. The secion also controls the button visibilty by linking to the and "RulesConfiguration" "Workflows" logic of when the ribbon element (usually button) is visible in the ribbon. Example Below is an example of two Mappings for the Party 1 and Background 1 styles buttons: XML from the Ribbon.xml file: <box id="PartyBox1" boxStyle="vertical"> <button id="PartyParties" image="Parties 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Parties" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> <button id="PartyBackground" image="Background 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Background" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> </box> JSON from the configuration file Scripts.json Note: the button id in the xml must match the name of the "Mappings" element name in the "Mappings" selction { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "ScriptConfiguration": { "Mappings": { "PartyParties": { "Script": "StyleParties", "EnabledWhen": "PartyGroupVisible", "VisibleWhen": "PartyGroupVisible" }, "PartyBackground": { "Script": "StyleBackground", "EnabledWhen": "PartyGroupVisible", "VisibleWhen": "PartyGroupVisible" }, There are three options of the "Mappings" element: "Script" - this is the name of the Script which is defined in the "Scripts": section (usually below the Mappings section of the "ScriptConfiguration" See the Scripts section below for a brief description. See the full documentation in Scripts.mp as there are a large number of options that can be used in the Scripts. "EnabledWhen" - this contains the name of the rules from "Rules" element of RulesConfiguration": { "Workflows": [{ "WorkflowName": "RibbonRules" See the EnabledWhen section below for a brief description. See the full documentation of RulesConfiguration.mp for documentation. "VisibleWhen" - this contains the name of the rules from "Rules" element of RulesConfiguration": { "Workflows": [{ "WorkflowName": "RibbonRules" See the EnabledWhen section below for a brief description. See the full documentation of RulesConfiguration.mp for documentation. "Scripts" The Scripts section is where the actions are defined when the ribbon button is pressed. See the full documentation in Scripts.md Example Scripts for the Party 1 and Background 1 buttons defined above: { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "ScriptConfiguration": { "Scripts": { "StyleParties": { "Comment": "Applies Parties Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Parties" } } ] }, "StyleBackground": { "Comment": "Applies Background Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Background" } } ] },

features/dms.html.title: DMS | User Docs
features/dms.html.summary: DMS The DMS.json file is for configuration of our Document Management System Integration. Schema Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Example Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. How to switch between different DMS options The K+B Word msi can integrate with different Document Management Systems (DMS). This is done using JSON configuration settings. For the DMS setting the JSON top level property "DocumentManagement": is used. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "DocumentManagement": { } } NB: "Provider" is the proptery for defining the DMS type Available DocumentManagement properties CreateOptions: this is used for debug testing PreProfileTemplates: (array) this holds the list of Templates to be pre-profiled (Front-end profiling). Each item can have the following properties: CloseOnCancel (Optional) DocType (Optional) Filter (Mandatory) Properties: ???What is this" PropertyMappings: contains the following 3 properties allowing information to be saved into the Word document: CustomProperties DocumentVariables DocumentProperties Provider: iManage, NetDocuments and None "PreProfileTemplates" properties This propery is used for listing the Word templates that are to be pre-profiled (Front-end profile). Filter: (Mandatory) this hold the name of the templates to be pre-profiled. It uses regular expressions. CloseOnCancel: true or false If set to true and the Cancel button is pressed on the DMS SaveAs screen then the initially created document is CLOSED. The default setting is false. DocType: used to pass to the DMS the document type to be set in the DMS SaveAs screen (currently only works in iManage - known bug in netDocs that information cannot be set in the SaveAs screen). **Example: ** A template called Letter is pre-profiled and if the cancel button is pressed then the initial document is closed. Any template that starts with "AB Company" is pre-profiled and if the cancel button is pressed then the document is not closed and the rest of the process (Filesite-New) is continued. "PreProfileTemplates": [ { "Filter": "Letter", "CloseOnCancel": true }, { "Filter": "AB Company*", "CloseOnCancel": false } ], "Properties" properties ??WHAT IS THIS USED FOR ??? "PropertyMappings" properties This property is used for defining the DMS information that is written into the Word document as either: Custom Document Properties, Variables or Document Properties. The three different properties are: CustomProperties, DocumentVariables and DocumentProperties. Each of these properties contain a list of paired values: "Name of variable": {{ DMSKeyName(s) and format }} "DocumentVariables": { "DMSAuthor": "{{ author.value }}", "DMSOurRef": "{{ profileInfo.identifier }}v{{ profileInfo.version | string.to_int | object.format \"d2\" }}" }, There can be multiple DMSKeyNames with additional joining text. The DMSKeyNames can be formatted using SCRIBAN formatting (https://github.com/scriban/scriban). Example: makes the document version into a 2 digit number but firstly converting the version string into an integer and then formatting this inro a 2 digit decimal number. "DMSOurRef": "{{ profileInfo.identifier }}v{{ profileInfo.version | string.to_int | object.format \"d2\" }}" "Provider" properties This property is used to set the current DMS system that is being used. There are three options: iManage: set to this option when iManage integration is required NetDocuments: set to this option when netDocs integration is required None: (default) set to this option when no DMS integration is required { "DocumentManagement": { "Provider": "NetDocuments", } } Vanilla iManage Net Docs Content for Vanilla... Content for iManage... DMSKeyNames The following DMSKEYNames can be used inf the "PropertyMappings" properties for saving into the Word document. Document Profile information profileInfo.identifier - Document number profileInfo.cabinet - profileInfo.version - Document version profileInfo.filename - Document file name profileInfo.lastModified - Date the document was last modified profileInfo.createdBy - Full name of the typist System information The other properties depend on how NetDocs is setup as will vary between different clients. View the New Document dialog box to look at the fields and values that need to be entered: On the left part of the screen are the list of fields these names can be used. Each has two elements .value and .description. Example: author.value eg EKT author.description eg Emma Thompson

features/scripts.html.title: Scripts - User Guide | User Docs
features/scripts.html.summary: Scripts Scripts allow you to automate multiple document editing operations with a single button click. Each script contains a sequence of commands that execute in order, helping you apply consistent formatting and maintain document standards efficiently. What are Scripts? Scripts are configurable automation tools that combine multiple editing commands into a single action. Rather than manually applying several formatting changes, you can create a script that applies all changes automatically to your selected text or entire document. Benefits Time-Saving: Execute multiple formatting operations with one click Consistency: Ensure uniform formatting across documents Reduced Errors: Eliminate manual formatting mistakes Customisable: Configure scripts to match your organisation's standards Reusable: Create once, use repeatedly across documents How is a Script activated? A Script can be activated via a button on the ribbon. Example ribbon button XML: <button id="OperativeLevel1Heading" image="Level 1 Heading" onAction="RunScript" label="Level 1 Heading" /> Notice that the onAction="RunScript" is being used. This is the command that is being run which will find the correct script to run using the button id="OperativeLevel1Heading". There is a JSON element ScriptConfiguration that needs to be set with the button mapping and the script instructions. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "ScriptConfiguration": { "Mappings": { "OperativeLevel1Heading": { "Script": "StyleLevel1Heading", "EnabledWhen": "OperativeGroupFullVisible", "VisibleWhen": "OperativeGroupFullVisible" } }, "Scripts": { "StyleLevel1Heading": { "Comment": "Applies Level 1 Heading Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Level 1 Heading" } } ] } } } } NB: The "Mappings": element is descriped in more details in ????? NB: The Mappings script name (text) in the line "Script": "StyleLevel1Heading", needs have a corresponding Scripts element with a matching name "StyleLevel1Heading": {. Creating a Script element A Script element is made up of three parts; a name and then two elements: Comment and Commands. Name - this needs to be unique and matches the Script name in the Mappings section of the JSON file "Comment": this element is only used for information by the person editting the JSON file as comments are not possible in JSON files. "Commands": this element is an array of Script commands that are run in sequence. There must be at least one script command eg to apply a style or a sequence of commands to perform a sequence of commands. "Scripts": { "StyleLevel1Heading": { "Comment": "Applies Level 1 Heading Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Level 1 Heading" } } ] } "Commands" elements A Commands element is made up of three parts; a name and then two elements: Comment and Commands. "$type" - this is the name of the script command. There are 12 types of command - Mandatory. "Range": - this holds the range that the script is going to affect within the current Word document. Some of the Command types do not require a Range option (Mandatory for some types). "Options": this element holds the different options available for the Command type. There is always at least one Options for all script commands. Available Script Commands types Scripts support 13 types of commands that can be combined in any sequence: Formatting Commands "$type" 1. Apply Font Formatting ("$type": "ApplyFontFormatting") Applies character-level formatting to text, including: Bold, italic, underline, doublestrikethrough, strikethrough CharacterCase Font colour and colour index All caps, small caps Superscript and subscript Text position Hidden text 2. Apply Paragraph Formatting ("$type": "ApplyParagraphFormatting") Applies paragraph-level formatting, including: Alignment (left, right, centre, justify) Indentation (left, right, first line) Line spacing and spacing before/after KeepWithNext, KeepWithNext, PageBreakBefore OutlineLevel, NoLineNumber Tab stops WindowControl 3. Apply Style ("$type": "ApplyStyle") Applies a named paragraph style to the selected text or range. NB: There are three types of Style: Paragraph, Character and Linked (Paragraph and Character) 4. Clear manually applied font formatting ("$type": "ClearFontFormatting") Clears all manually applied font formatting to the selection or range - equivalent to Ctrl+Spacebar 5. Clear manually applied paragraph formatting ("$type": "ClearParagraphFormatting") Clears all manually applied paragraph formatting to the selection or range. Text Manipulation Commands 6. Delete Characters ("$type": "DeleteCharacters") Removes specified characters from the text. Useful for cleaning up unwanted characters or formatting marks. 7. Delete Words ("$type": "DeleteWords") Removes specified characters from the text. Useful for cleaning up unwanted characters or formatting marks. 8. Enforce Case ("$type": "EnforceCase") Converts text to a specific case: Uppercase (ALL CAPS) Lowercase (all lowercase) Title Case (First Letter Capital) Sentence case (First letter of sentence) 9. Insert Field ("$type": "InsertField") Inserts a Word field (such as page numbers, dates, or document properties) at the specified location. 10. Insert Word ("$type": "InsertWord") Inserts specified text at the cursor position or within a range. Can optionally prevent duplicate insertions. Custom Document Property Manipulation Commands 11. Set the value of a Custom Document Property ("$type": "SetCustomProperty") Used to set the value of a Custom Document Property. These can be used to show or hide buttons for groups on the ribbon. Use in tandem with UnsetCustomProperty 12. Delete a Custom Document Property ("$type": "DeleteCustomProperty") Used to unset the value of a Custom Document Property. These can be used to show or hide buttons for groups on the ribbon. Use in tandem with SetCustomProperty VBA Macro Commands 13. Run VBA Macro ("$type": "RunMacro") Executes an existing VBA macro by name with optional parameters. This allows you to integrate existing VBA automation with your script configurations. Use Cases: Execute legacy VBA macros from ribbon buttons Run document-specific macros stored in templates Trigger complex automation that's already implemented in VBA ??. Delete Text / Replace Text???? (Not yet available) Removes specified characters from the text. Useful for cleaning up unwanted characters or formatting marks. Using Scripts Scripts appear as buttons in your Word ribbon. The visibility and availability of scripts are controlled by configuration and can be customised per user or organisation. Executing a Script Select text (if the script applies to a selection) or position your cursor Click the script button in the ribbon The script executes all commands in sequence Changes are applied to your document Tip Scripts respect Word's undo stack. If you need to reverse script changes, press Ctrl+Z immediately after execution. Script Behaviour Sequential Execution: Commands execute in the order configured Range Application: Each command can target different ranges: Entire document ("CommandRange": "EntireDocument") - "THIS DOES NOT EXIST IN THE SCHEMEA AT THE MOMENT SO NOT AVAILABLE" Current selection ("CommandRange": "Selection") All paragraphs of selection ("CommandRange": "Paragraphs") First paragraph of selection ("CommandRange": "FirstParagraph") Last paragraph of selection ("CommandRange": "LastParagraph") SubRange Application: the following Range options can also have a SubRange ("CommandRange": "Selection") and : Selection ("CommandRange": "Selection") with sub-ranges (first word, last word, all words, first sentence, last sentence, all sentences) Current selection ("CommandRange": "Selection") Error Handling: If a command fails, script execution stops, and you receive an error message Configuration Scripts are configured through JSON files stored in your application data directory: Shared Configuration: %appdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config User Configuration: %localappdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config JSON Schema The complete JSON Schema definition for script configuration: Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Tip Use this schema in your JSON editor for IntelliSense and validation. Most editors (VS Code, Visual Studio, etc.) support JSON Schema auto-completion when you add a $schema property: { "$schema": "path/to/ScriptConfiguration.schema.json", "Scripts": { ... } } Script Structure Each script requires: Name: Unique identifier for the script Description: Plain-language explanation of what the script does Commands: List of operations to perform Mapping: Controls visibility and availability in the ribbon Configuration Example { "Scripts": { "FormatLegalDocument": { "Comment": "Applies standard legal document formatting", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "CommandRange": "AllParagraphs", "StyleName": "Legal Body Text" }, { "$type": "ApplyFontFormatting", "CommandRange": "Selection", "SubRange": "All", "Bold": 0, "Italic": 0 }, { "$type": "DeleteCharacters", "CommandRange": "EntireDocument", "SubRange": "All", "Characters": " " } ] } }, "Mappings": { "formatLegalDoc": { "Script": "FormatLegalDocument", "EnabledWhen": "Always", "VisibleWhen": "Always" } } } Configuration Options EnabledWhen Always: Script is always available Custom rules expression (advanced) VisibleWhen Always: Script button always visible in ribbon Custom rules expression (advanced) CommandRange Options (Is the attribute mandatory ??? Check with Phill EntireDocument: Applies to whole document ("CommandRange": "EntireDocument") Selection: Applies to currently selected text ("CommandRange": "Selection") Paragraphs: Applies to every paragraph in the selection ("CommandRange": "Paragraphs") FirstParagraph: Applies to first paragraph only in the selection ("CommandRange": "FirstParagraph") LastParagraph: Applies to last paragraph only in the selection ("CommandRange": "LastParagraph") SubRange Options (for granular control) All: Entire range FirstWord: First word only LastWord: Last word only Available on InsertText only Start: First word only End: Last word only Command Types Reference 1. ApplyFontFormatting Mandatory Properties: Ranges: $type: EntireDocument, FirstParagraph, LastParagraph, Paragraphs, Selection SubRange: All, FirstWord, LastWord Options: There needs to be at least one option for the Script to do anything NB: EntireDocument applies to the Main story in the document - it dose not include text in a textbox in the main story NB: The ("$type": "ClearFontFormatting") can be used to remove all manual formatting prior to running font formatting commands "Comment": "Testing Applying Bold", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyFontFormatting", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "Bold": "True" } } ] Available Options: at least one must be used for the script to do anything: Bold, Italic: False, Toggle, True CharacterCase: FullWidth, HalfWidth, LowerCase, NextCase, TitleSentence, TitleWord, ToggleCase, UpperCase AllCaps, SmallCaps: False, Toggle, True Underline: None, Single, Double, Dotted, etc. Colour: Text string that can either be the RGB colour value enters as a text string eg RGB(0,0,255) or the hex value eg #1234AB ColourIndex: 0 - Not sure how this is used ????? Hidden: False, Toggle, True Position: Raise/lower text (in points) Subscript, Superscript: False, Toggle, True StrikeThrough, DoubleStrikeThrough: False, Toggle, True 2. ApplyParagraphFormatting Mandatory Properties: Ranges: $type: EntireDocument, FirstParagraph, LastParagraph, Paragraphs, Selection Options: There needs to be at least one option for the Script to do anything NB: There is no SubType option in the Range for ApplyParagraphFormatting as it applies to whole paragraphs. "Comment": "Testing removing space after", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyParagraphFormatting", "Range": { "$type": "Selection" }, "Options": { "SpaceAfter": 0 } } ] Available Options at least one must be used for the script to do anything: Alignment: Center, Justified, Left, Right BaseLineAlignment: Baseline, Center, FarEast50, Top CharacterUnitFirstLineIndent, CharacterUnitLeftIndent, CharacterUnitRightIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) FirstLineIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) KeepTogether, KeepWithNext: False, True, Undefined LeftIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) LineSpacing: 0 value in points (always +ve?) LineSpacingRule: AtLeast, Double, Exactly, Multiple, OnePointFive, Single LineUnitAfter, LineUnitBefore: 0 value in points (always +ve?) MirrorIndents: False, True, Undefined NoLineNumber: False, True, Undefined OutlineLevel: BodyText, Level1, Level2, . . . Level9 PageBreakBefore: False, True, Undefined RightIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) SpaceAfter, SpaceBefore: 0 value in points (always +ve?) WidowControl: False, True, Undefined 3. Apply Selection Formatting $type: ApplySelectionFormatting, CommandRanges: EntireDocument, FirstParagraph, LastParagraph, Paragraphs, Selection SubRange: Not used - the default option is All StyleName:

features/utilities.html.title: Utilities | User Docs
features/utilities.html.summary: Utilities The Utilities group contains a set of tools designed to make document management and formatting more efficient and consistent. These tools help you maintain style standards, manage templates, and apply common settings with just a few clicks, ensuring that your documents remain professional and aligned with your organization's formatting rules. NB: This is a standard Word idMso command Toggle Paragraph Marks (idMso ParagraphMarks) Known as "Show/Hide ¶" in native Word, the Toggle Paragraph Marks feature in Word, often represented by the ¶ symbol, is used to show or hide non-printing characters in your document. These characters are invisible when you print or view the document normally but can affect spacing, layout, and formatting. What it shows: When you toggle it on, Word displays symbols for: Paragraph breaks (¶): Marks the end of a paragraph. Spaces (·): Small dots showing spaces between words. Tabs (→): Arrows showing where tab stops are used. Line breaks (↵): Indicates a manual line break without starting a new paragraph. Hidden text: Text formatted as hidden will also appear. Why it’s useful: Troubleshooting formatting issues: If spacing looks off or alignment isn’t right, these markers help you see exactly what’s happening. Editing complex documents: Shows where extra spaces, tabs, or paragraph marks might be affecting layout. Consistent formatting: Ensures that elements like tables, lists, and indents are structured correctly. see more at https://support.microsoft.com/en-gb/office/show-or-hide-tab-marks-in-word-84a53213-5d02-404a-b022-09cae1a3958b Ribbon.xml <toggleButton idMso="ParagraphMarks" /> Clear Text Formatting (Command ClearTextFormatting) The Clear Text Formatting tool removes all manual character-level formatting from the selected text. Note: This is equivalent to pressing the Control+Spacebar shortcut key on the keyboard - but there is no in-built ClearFontFormatting button in Word to add to the Quick Access Toolbar! This is useful when text has been copied from other sources or heavily edited and contains inconsistent font styling. Strips away character-specific formatting, such as: Font type, size, and colour Bold, italic, underline, strikethrough Highlighting and text effects Leaves the plain text content intact This is often used in conjuction with the Clear Paragraph Formatting tool. Note: this does not remove colour applied using the "Text Highlight Colour" tool - this is by design (by Microsoft), Word considers it a display option. Usage The tool only applies to the current selected text - if only part of a paragraph is selected then applies to the selected text ONLY. Ribbon.xml <button id="ClearTextFormatting" onAction="ClearTextFormatting" label="Clear Manual Text Formatting" showLabel="false" image="ClearTextMarking" screentip="Clear Manual Text Formatting" supertip="Clear additional manual text formatting. Any changes to the text other than that defined by the style will be removed." /> Note: that some older .xml files call a function RemoveTextFormatting - this will not work. Clear Paragraph Formatting (Command ClearParagraphFormatting) The Clear Paragraph Formatting tool removes all manual paragraph-level formatting from the selected text, restoring it to the formatting defined by the applied style. Note: This is equivalent to pressing the Control+Q shortcut key on the keyboard - but there is no in-built ClearParagraphFormatting button in Word to add to the Quick Access Toolbar! This is useful when text has inconsistent or unwanted formatting applied using the Paragraph Formatting options in Word. Strips away paragraph-specific formatting, such as: Indents (left, right, first line) Line spacing and spacing before/after paragraphs Alignment (left, right, center, justify) Tabs and paragraph-level borders/shading Keeps the text content and character formatting (like bold or italic) intact This is often used in conjuction with the Clear Text Formatting tool. Usage The tool only applies to the current selection - if only part of a paragraph is selected then applies to the whole paragraph as this is paragraph formatting. Ribbon.xml <button id="ClearParagraphFormatting" onAction="ClearParagraphFormatting" label="Clear Paragraph Formatting" showLabel="false" image="ClearParagraphMarking" screentip="Clear Manual Paragraph Formatting" supertip="Clear additional manual paragraph formatting. Any changes to the paragraph other than that defined by the style will be removed." /> Note: that some older .xml files call a function RemoveTextFormattingAll - this will not work. Toggle Navigation Pane (idMso DocumentMapReadingView) The Navigation pane is a built-in tool in Microsoft Word that helps you quickly browse, search, and reorganise the content of your document. It’s especially useful in large or structured documents with headings. Headings View Displays a list of all headings in the document, based on styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, etc.) Lets you jump directly to any section by clicking the heading Supports drag-and-drop to reorder sections, which moves both the heading and its associated content Page Thumbnails View Shows small thumbnail previews of each page Helps you visually navigate to specific pages quickly Search (Results) View Lets you search for words or phrases in the document Displays a list of all results; clicking a result jumps to its location Usage The tool only applies to the current selected document. This command needs to be used on a per document basis. Ribbon.xml Note: The icon has been changed to the standard one we use in the ribbon <button idMso="DocumentMapReadingView" showLabel="false" imageMso="RmsNavigationBar" /> Toggle Table Grid Lines The View Table Gridlines feature in Microsoft Word lets you see the structure of tables that don’t have visible borders. It shows dotted lines where table cell boundaries are, making it easier to work with the layout even if no borders are applied. Ribbon.xml <toggleButton idMso="TableShowGridlines" showLabel="false" /> Toggle Print/Draft View Provides a convenient way to switch between Draft view and Print Layout view Draft View in Microsoft Word is a document view designed to help you focus on writing and editing text content without being distracted by complex layout elements. It prioritizes speed and simplicity over precise page appearance. It also clearly displays the Paragraph styles used. Print Layout view in Microsoft Word shows your document exactly as it will appear on the printed page. It is the default and most commonly used view, designed for working on the visual formatting and layout of your document. Ribbon.xml <button id="TogglePrintDraft" onAction="TogglePrintDraftView" label="Draft/Print view" showLabel="false" imageMso="Zoom100" screentip="Toggle Print/Draft View" supertip="Toggle between Print View and Draft View." /> Delete Double Paragraphs Removes unnecessary empty paragraphs (blank lines) from your document, improving structure and readability. By doing so, paragraph styles can function as intended, ensuring consistent formatting throughout the document in line with company branding. WHY THIS SHOULD BE "EXTRA" RETURNS ALC - Also add command details Usage The tool first processes the selected text and then prompts you to check the rest of the document. If it reaches the end, it will ask whether you want to start from the beginning. After completing the operation, it reports the total number of replacements made. Ribbon.xml <button id="deleteDoubleParagraphsButton" onAction="DeleteDoubleParagraphs" screentip="Delete Double Paragraphs" label="Delete Double Paragraphs" showLabel="false" image="DoubleParaDelete" /> Delete Double Spaces Removes unnecessary spaces from your document, this ensures consistent spacing, improves readability and helps maintain a professional layout. Usage The tool first processes the selected text and then prompts you to check the rest of the document. If it reaches the end, it will ask whether you want to start from the beginning. After completing the operation, it reports the total number of replacements made. Ribbon.xml <button id="deleteDoubleSpacesButton" onAction="DeleteDoubleSpaces" label="Delete Double Spaces" showLabel="false" screentip="Delete Double Spaces" image="DeleteDoubleSpaces" /> Smart Quotes The Smart Quotes tool helps you manage quotation marks consistently throughout your document. Toggle Smart Quotes A toggle button to turn Word’s Smart Quotes feature on or off globally. When enabled, Word automatically converts straight quotes (") to typographic (curly) quotes (“”) as you type. Convert to Smart Quotes Converts all quotation marks in the selected text to Smart Quotes. Useful for cleaning up pasted text or documents created with straight quotes. Convert to Straight Quotes Converts all quotation marks in the selected text to straight quotes ("). Useful for coding, data entry, or formatting for plain text environments. Usage The tool first processes the selected text and then prompts you to check the rest of the document. If it reaches the end, it will ask whether you want to start from the beginning. After completing the operation, it reports the total number of replacements made. Ribbon.xml <splitButton id="SmartQuotesSplit" size="normal" showLabel="false"> <toggleButton id="SmartQuotesToggle" onAction="ToggleSmartQuotes" label="Smart Quotes" showLabel="false" image="Quotes" screentip="Toggle Smart Quotes" supertip="Toggle the use of smart quotes in the document." /> <menu id="SmartQuotesMenu"> <button id="SmartQuotes" onAction="SmartQuotes" label="Smart Quotes" showLabel="false" screentip="Switch from straight quotes to Smart quotes." image="CurlyQuotes" /> <button id="StraightQuotes" onAction="StraightQuotes" label="Straight Quotes" showLabel="false" screentip="Switch from Smart quotes to Straight quotes." image="StraightQuotes" /> </menu> </splitButton> Reset Styles The Reset Styles tool in restores all styles in your document to match the original styles defined in the template it was based on. This ensures consistent formatting and helps maintain adherence to company or template standards. Ribbon.xml <button id="ResetStyles" onAction="ResetStyles" label="Reset Styles" showLabel="false" visible="true" imageMso="StylesDialogClassic" screentip="Reset Paragraph Styles to default settings" /> AttachTemplate The Attach Template tool lets you review and modify the template attached to the current document. It provides options to redefine the default template and optionally reset the document’s styles to match that template. Example dialog box Currently attached template: - displays the name of the template that is currently attached the to the selected document. Default template to attach: - the contents (the template name) of the custom document property TemplatePropertyName is displayed. The property TemplatePropertyName contains the Custom Document Property name that this information is saved into if not set then the default K+BTemplate is used. This setting is used by the command AttachTemplateOnOpen which checks the correct template is attached to the document on the DocumentOpen event. List of available templates: This list the available templates. See How the templates to be displayed are found below for the file location and the templates that are listed. The templates that are listed show three further bits of information: Version - this is picked up from the Custom Document properties of the document. This will use the following sequence of custom document properties read in the following order: K+BVersion, BrochetVersion, TemplafyVersion (need to double check that this name) Date - this is picked up from the Custom Document properties of the document. This will use the following sequence of custom document properties read in the following order: K+BDate, BrochetDate, TemplafyDate (need to double check that this name) Additional information - this is picked up from the Comments field on the document properties (NOTE: not K+BComments) Highlighted template in the List of avilable templates: Highlights the template name which is currently attached to the document (e.g., Normal.dotm). NOTE: Git# 225 - this request is to where the Version and Date is read: eg for Version reads in the following order and stops as soon as information is found:K+BVersion -> BrochetVersion -> TemplafyVersion. NOTE: Git# 227 - Request for a loading symbol when AttachedTemplates have been pressed and before the dialog box comes up. NOTE: Git# 230 - The documents attached template is highlighted in the list but is not visible is low down in the list - need to scroll the list to make it visible NOTE: Git# 231 - Using on a Word template - should not display an attached template - should not allow the Reset Styles option How the templates to be displayed are found See the Scheme below. The following options can be set SearchForTemplateName - default setting is Normal.dotm. This is the template name that is looked for in the following locations: User Templates then Workgroup Template. Once found this is the templates location that is used to source all the templates listed in the listbox on the dialog box. If the template name is not found in either of the subdirectories then the default setting is used (Normal.dotm) Which templates are displayed in the listbox See the Scheme below. The following options can be set IncludeFilter - default setting is "^.*\.dot[mx]\(". This uses regular expressions to determine the templates to be listed. Example: "^Test.*\\.dot[mx]\) will only list templates which start with the text "Test" eg Testing 1.dotx, Testing 2.dotx. ExcludeFilter - default setting is "". This uses regular expressions to determine the templates to be excluded from the list. All temporary Word template files (ones that start with ~ are excluded as default). Key User Features User selects one template that is to be attached to the selected document (highlighted in the list) User ticks/unticks the **Reset default template name** option User ticks/unticks the **Reset document styles** option ON PRESSING OK **Always on pressing OK** sets the selected document to have the selected template as the attached template. **Reset default template name** if ticked saves the selected template name into the custom document property defined by the property "TemplatePropertyName". The default setting is "K+BTemplate". **Reset document styles** if ticked resets all styles in the document to match the attached template. Default config JSON "AttachTemplateConfiguration": { "SearchForTemplateName": "Normal.dotm", "IncludeFilter": "^.*\\.dot[mx]$", "ExcludeFilter": "", "TemplatePropertyName": "K+BTemplate" } PROPERTY OPTIONS "SearchForTemplateName" - this is the name of the template the code searches for to determine which templates location to use eg User Templates or Workgroup Templates. "IncludeFilter" - this is a regular expression that will list the templates to be displayed in the listbox. "ExcludeFilter" - this is a regular expression that will list the templates NOT to be displayed in the listbox. "TemplatePropertyName" - this is the name of the custom document property that holds the default template name. This information is used on the ??? option which makes sure the correct template is attached to the document. This is useful for Templafy clients or when a document is sent outside the firm and is returned with the document attached to Normal.dotm. Ribbon.xml <button id="AttachTemplate" onAction="AttachTemplate" label="Check Template" showLabel="false" visible="true" screentip="Check Template attached to Current Document" supertip="Displays a dialog box showing the Word template that is currently attached to the document." imageMso="AttachMenu" /> Configuration Schema Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Example Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. AttachTemplateOnOpen This is a command that is run on the Doucment Open Event. If the configuration setting AttachTemplateOnOpen is set to True then when a Document is opened the value in the Custom Document Property K+BTemplate is used as the Word template name to attach. The name of the custom doucment property can be changed by setting the configuration setting Default config JSON "AttachTemplateConfiguration": { "AttachTemplateOnOpen": false, "TemplatePropertyName": "K+BTemplate" }

Schema Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Example Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Properties LicenseId Must be a valid GUID ActivationId

licensing/overview.html.title: Licensing | User Docs
licensing/overview.html.summary: Licensing If there is a valid Licence then the application will run in full mode. If there is no valid licence then the application will run in a limited mode. Activating a Licence Open the About Dialog from the Ribbon and click 'Activate'. Deactivating a Licence Open the About Dialog from the Ribbon and click 'Deactivate'.
